sources.notes: Read the row-shift operation, right/down path cost, and bounds n,m<=200 with total n*m<=5e4.
The official editorial defines f(i,j) and shift-aware g(i,j,s), including the O(nm^2) recurrence and final minimum over shifts.

The opened Luogu write-up independently models shift as an expanded shortest-path state, presents an accepted Dijkstra implementation, and notes that the state graph can instead be relaxed as a DAG DP.
Re-derived when shift cost is charged and matched the O(nm^2) recurrence against exhaustive row shifts and all monotone paths on 1280 random grids with 1<=n,m<=4.

annotation.summary: Choose a cyclic left shift for every grid row, paying k per unit shift, and then find a minimum-cost right/down path from (1,1) to (n,m) in the shifted grid.
annotation.constraints: Up to 1e4 tests; 1 <= n,m <= 200, 0 <= k,a[i][j] <= 1e9, and sum(n*m) <= 5e4. The O(nm^2) bound is viable, and all costs must use 64-bit integers.
annotation.core_idea: Only shifts s in [0,m-1] matter because m shifts restore a row and have nonnegative extra cost. Let prev[j] be the minimum cost at column j after processing the previous row. For a fixed shift s of row i, scan columns left to right and set g_s[j] = a[i][(j+s) mod m] + min(prev[j] + k*s, g_s[j-1]). The first predecessor enters this row from above and pays its shift exactly once; the second continues right in the same already-shifted row. After scanning all shifts, cur[j] = min_s g_s[j]. Initialize prev[0]=0 and all other entries to infinity before the first row; after n rows the answer is prev[m-1].
annotation.complexity: O(nm^2) time and O(m) working DP memory, excluding storage of the input grid
tricks: Reduce an arbitrary number of row shifts to residues 0..m-1 because shift cost is nonnegative.
Keep the chosen shift only inside the current-row state and minimize it away at the row boundary.
Charge k*s only on the transition entering a row, never again on horizontal continuation.
Use 64-bit infinity because both path values and accumulated shift costs can exceed 32-bit range.

solution_variants.name: row-shift-augmented-grid-dp
solution_variants.summary: For each row and each shift residue, perform a left-to-right DP combining entry from the previous row with horizontal continuation, then minimize over shifts for every column.
solution_variants.complexity: O(nm^2) time and O(m) working memory

solution_variants.name: expanded-state-shortest-path
solution_variants.summary: Create shift-layer states G(i,j,s), collapsed states F(i,j), horizontal edges that preserve s, and vertical-entry edges that choose s and pay k*s. The graph is acyclic and admits O(nm^2) topological relaxation; a generic nonnegative-edge Dijkstra implementation is an accepted O(nm^2 log(nm^2)) alternative documented by the Luogu source.
solution_variants.complexity: O(nm^2) as DAG shortest path, or O(nm^2 log(nm^2)) with Dijkstra; O(nm^2) explicit-state memory

tags.evidence: The optimum is propagated through right/down grid positions row by row, with transitions from the cell above or the previous column.
A cell coordinate alone is insufficient inside a row; the DP must additionally remember that row's chosen cyclic-shift residue so all horizontal cells use the same transformed row.
The intended complexity comes from exhaustively trying all m possible shift residues for each row while DP handles all path choices.
With collapsed row-boundary nodes and shift-layer nodes, every transition advances the row or column order, so the expanded shortest-path graph is a DAG.
The consulted Luogu alternative runs Dijkstra on the nonnegative expanded shift-state graph and is materially distinct from the compact row DP implementation.
Modulo indexing of shifted rows, large infinity values, and charging each row shift once are the main implementation hazards.
